coredata.dc:title: Highly selective and sensitive detection of trace amoxicillin with a novel fluorescent nanoprobe N, S-CDs@MIP based on S/N co-doped carbon quantum dots combined with molecularly imprinted polymer

coredata.dc:description: At present, the rapid, sensitive, and highly selective detection of trace organic contaminants is a research spot in the field of environment. Herein, a novel fluorescent nanoprobe N, S-CDs@MIP based on N/S co-doped carbon quantum dots (CDs) coupled with amoxicillin molecularly imprinted polymer was synthesized. The fluorescent property of N, S-CDs@MIP and fluorescence quenching level of amoxicillin to N, S-CDs@MIP was explored. Furthermore, the fluorescence analysis method based on N, S-CDs@MIP probe for quantitatively detecting amoxicillin was established. The synthesized N, S-CDs@MIP has a maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of 440 nm and a significantly selective fluorescence response toward amoxicillin with a large imprinting factor of 4.07. For the developed fluorescence analysis method, the detection limit, the linear range of the standard curve, and relative standard deviation (RSD) were obtained as 1.17 ng/mL, 5.8–200 ng/mL, and <3 %, respectively. The real environmental water samples were measured with a high accuracy with the recovery of 83.6 % ~ 104.7 % and RSD of 2.3 % ~ 5.1 %. Compared to the HPLC-UV, HPLC-MS, and the fluorescence detection method based on CDs or doped CDs, the established fluorescence detection method exhibits high selectivity, wide linear range, and low detection limit. It is greatly promising in the quick detection of trace amoxicillin.

head.ce:title: Highly selective and sensitive detection of trace amoxicillin with a novel fluorescent nanoprobe N, S-CDs@MIP based on S/N co-doped carbon quantum dots combined with molecularly imprinted polymer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, 212100, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: At present, the rapid, sensitive, and highly selective detection of trace organic contaminants is a research spot in the field of environment. Herein, a novel fluorescent nanoprobe N, S-CDs@MIP based on N/S co-doped carbon quantum dots (CDs) coupled with amoxicillin molecularly imprinted polymer was synthesized. The fluorescent property of N, S-CDs@MIP and fluorescence quenching level of amoxicillin to N, S-CDs@MIP was explored. Furthermore, the fluorescence analysis method based on N, S-CDs@MIP probe for quantitatively detecting amoxicillin was established. The synthesized N, S-CDs@MIP has a maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of 440 nm and a significantly selective fluorescence response toward amoxicillin with a large imprinting factor of 4.07. For the developed fluorescence analysis method, the detection limit, the linear range of the standard curve, and relative standard deviation (RSD) were obtained as 1.17 ng/mL, 5.8–200 ng/mL, and <3 %, respectively. The real environmental water samples were measured with a high accuracy with the recovery of 83.6 % ~ 104.7 % and RSD of 2.3 % ~ 5.1 %. Compared to the HPLC-UV, HPLC-MS, and the fluorescence detection method based on CDs or doped CDs, the established fluorescence detection method exhibits high selectivity, wide linear range, and low detection limit. It is greatly promising in the quick detection of trace amoxicillin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, antibiotics have been extensively applied in treating bacterial infections in human, animal husbandry, and poultry farming [1], which makes antibiotics become one category of emerging pollutants [2]. Amoxicillin (AMX), as a penicillin antibiotic, is widely used in the therapy of infections resulting from gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria in human bodies and the prevention of animal diseases in animal husbandry and aquaculture [3]. Because of its low metabolic rate, 70–90 % of the amoxicillin applied in humans and livestock was released into the environment through urine and feces [4]. Up to now, amoxicillin has been detected in drinking water, milk, hospital waste, fish, food, influent in sewage-treatment plants, and natural water bodies [5–9]. The content of amoxicillin in different water bodies is in the range of 0.18–4.57 μgL−1 [10]. It has been reported that the presence of amoxicillin in the environment can cause the emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria and danger to ecosystem and the humans [11]. Furthermore, amoxicillin has the characteristics of biomagnification along the food chain. Therefore, it is an urgent task to monitor the amoxicillin in the environment. Nowadays, the conventional analysis methods of amoxicillin include HPLC [12], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [13], capillary electrophoresis [14], and FT-IR spectrometry [15]. These traditional methods suffer from some drawbacks including time-consuming sample preparation, complex separation operation, high cost, low sensitivity, and matrix interference. In recent decades, some sensitive, fast, and convenient analysis methods have been built to measure amoxicillin, including surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [16], microbiological methods [17], electrochemical methods [18], and fluorescence analysis [19]. Among these, the fluorescence-based analysis method is considered a promising method for the quantitative determination of metal and organics due to its high sensitivity, simple operation, economical instrumentation, and environmental friendliness [20].

Fluorescent nanoprobes are a class of light-emitting nanomaterials. They can interact with metal ions or organics, that is, the subject-guest interaction, thereby causing changes in fluorescence signals to achieve selective analysis and identification of target compounds [21]. Up to now, organic dye, carbon dots (CDs), and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have been used as fluorescence probes [22,23]. Compared to organic dyes and QDs, CDs have the merits of good biocompatibility, low toxicity, high fluorescence stability, easy fluorescence regulation, and environmental friendliness [24], Thus, they are widely applied in many fields such as chemical sensing, drug delivery, and bioimaging [25–28]. Nevertheless, the pristine CDs possess the disadvantage of relatively low fluorescence quantum yield [29]. The heteroatom doping is a simple and efficient strategy to improve the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs. The doped CDs possess higher fluorescence intensity as well as more active sites that can improve the affinity of carbon dots to the target molecules [30,31]. Of various heteroatom-doped CDs, S/N co-doped CDs exhibit higher fluorescence quantum yields, superior fluorescence properties, and good water solubility [32,33]. In the previous report, the pristine and doped CDs have been used in the quantitative analysis of amoxicillin. For example, Guo et al. [34] synthesized S/N co-doped CDs and used it in the quantitative detection of amoxicillin, realizing a linear range of 5 to 80 μmol/L as well as a detection limit of 3.46 μmol/L. Zhang et al. [35] employed a boron-doped CDs as a fluorescence probe to determine amoxicillin, achieving the linear range of 1.43–429.12 μmol/L as well as the detection limit of 0.825 μmol/L. The Cu/N co-doped CDs synthesized by Mahmoud et al. showed a detection limit of 0.06 μmol/L for the detection of amoxicillin in the range of 0.2–120.0 μmol/L [36]. However, the above-mentioned doped CDs have a higher detection limit and lower selectivity for the detection of amoxicillin. Therefore, it is of interest to explore a new fluorescence probe with lower detection limit and higher selectivity for the quantitative analysis of amoxicillin.

The molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have good specific recognition ability toward the template molecules owing to the presence of binding sites matched with the template molecules [37,38]. They have received increasing attention in target compound separation [39], analytical sensors [40], and catalysis [41]. In particular, MIPs have been coupled with carbon dots for fluorescence analysis. Up to now, only a few of fluorescence probes based on CDs coupled with MIPs have been reported. For example, Hu et al. [28] embedded Mg, N-CDs into MIP, developing a new sensitive fluorescent nano-sensor for detecting tetracycline in animal-derived food. Li et al. [42] reported a CDs and dye-doped MIP fluorescence sensor for determining dimethoate. Marziye et al. [43] developed a CDs embedded molecularly imprinted silica and put it into use in fluorescence probe for the analysis of acetamidine. Unfortunately, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report on fluorescent probes based on N/S co-doped CDs coupled with MIPs for the trace analysis of amoxicillin.

Inspired by the previous report, in this work, we integrate the merits of the strong fluorescence signal of the N, S co-doped CDs and high specific recognition of amoxicillin molecularly imprinted polymer, developing an ultra-sensitive fluorescence nanoprobe (N, S-CDs@MIP) for the quantitative detection of amoxicillin. N, S-CDs@MIP shows specific recognition and highly sensitive fluorescence response to amoxicillin. The fluorescence detection method of amoxicillin with N, S-CDs@MIP as the probe was established. Compared to the conventional detection method for amoxicillin, the developed fluorescence detection method exhibits the advantages of high selectivity, low detection limit, no complex pretreatments, and easy operation. It is a promising analysis technique for the quick determination of trace amoxicillin. This work provides a new strategy for creating a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence analysis method applied to detecting trace organic contaminants from the environment.

Ammonium citrate (AC), methanol (≥99.5 %), glacial acetic acid (≥99.5 %), Na2S2O3·5H2O (≥99.5 %), (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O (≥99.5 %), NH4Fe(SO4)2·12H2O (≥99.5 %), ZnSO4·7H2O (≥99.5 %), CaCO3 (≥99.5 %), KCl (≥99.5 %), MgO (99.9 %), Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (≥99.0 %), NaCl (≥99.5 %) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). Methacrylate (MAA, ≥98.0 %), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA, ≥98.0 %), azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN, ≥98.0 %), and amoxicillin were provided by Aladdin Co. Ltd. (China). Ampicillin (98 %), ofloxacin (98 %), tetracycline (98 %), chloramphenicol (98 %), and ciprofloxacin (98 %) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (China).

N, S-CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method. Ammonium citrate (AC) was employed as the nitrogen and carbon source, and sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate (Na2S2O3·5H2O) was used as the sulfur source. Specifically, 5 mmol of ammonium citrate and 5 mmol of sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate were separately added to 50 mL of ultrapure water and were agitated for dissolution and thorough mixing. The resulting mixture was then put into a 100 mL reaction flask. The reaction was conducted at 180 °C for 6 h in an oven. At the end of the reaction, the obtained suspension was centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to get rid of the precipitates. Then, the obtained supernatant was dialyzed using dialysis membranes (molecular weight cutoff of 500) for 24 h to take out impurities. Finally, the purified N, S-CDs solution was freeze-dried at −50 °C for 24 h. The solid N, S-CDs were obtained.

Amoxicillin (0.0731 g) was added to a 50 mL round-bottom flask. Afterward, 15 mL of methanol and 1 mL of ultrapure water were put into the flask, and the resulting mixture was sonicated until the amoxicillin was completely dissolved. Under stirring, 8 mg of the as-prepared N, S-CDs and 0.1378 g of methacrylic acid (MAA) were sequentially introduced to the flask. After agitation of 30 min, 1.5858 g of EGDMA and 25 mg of initiator AIBN were added to the flask in sequence. After sonication for degassing, a reflux condenser was installed, and nitrogen gas was used to purge the oxygen from the reaction system. The polymerization reaction was conducted in an oil-bath of 60 °C for 20 h under the condition of a stirring speed of 300 rpm. The resulting precipitates were rinsed several times with methanol and were then extracted in a Soxhlet extractor for 24 h with the mixture containing acetic acid and methanol (volume ratio of 1:9) as a solvent to elute the template molecule amoxicillin. Finally, the product was washed with methanol and dried for 24 h at 50 °C to obtain N, S-CDs@MIP. In contrast, non-molecularly imprinted polymer (NIP) N, S-CDs@NIP was synthesized in the same way without the presence of the template molecule amoxicillin. The fabrication procedure of N, S-CDs@MIP and the sensing mechanism for the fluorescence detection of amoxicillin is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of N, S-CDs@MIP were observed on a Regulus 8100 field emission transmission electron microscopy (Hitachi, Japan). The TEM and HRTEM images of N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs@MIP were obtained from a Talos F200X transmission electron microscope (FEI Company, USA). The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded on a Nicolet iS20 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

The amoxicillin solution (or real water sample) (5 mL) and 5 mg N, S-CDs@MIP were added to a cuvette, respectively. After ultrasonic dispersion and incubation of 45 min, the fluorescence spectrum was measured on an FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, England). The fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@MIP to amoxicillin or its analogues is expressed by F0/F. Where, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of MIP at the maximum excitation wavelength (355 nm), and F indicates the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs@MIP bound with amoxicillin or its analogues at 355 nm.

To evaluate the feasibility and accuracy of the fluorescent analysis method developed in this work for practical sample analysis, the water samples from the Yangtze River were selected as real samples. The collected water sample was filtered via a 0.45 μm filtration membrane to take out the suspended solid. The filtered water sample was used for the fluorescence measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image (Fig. 1a) of N, S-CDs indicates that N, S-CDs present a well-dispersed spherical shape with a dimeter of approximately 3 nm. Additionally, From the HRTEM image (Fig. 1b), the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was clearly observed, which is attributed to the interplanar spacing of the (100) plane of sp2-hybridized carbon materials [44]. The above results confirm the successful preparation of CDs. As seen in the SEM image (Fig. 1c) and TEM image (Fig. 1d) of N, S-CDs@MIP, the N, S-CDs@MIP appeared as microspheres with a dimeter of 200–300 nm. Moreover, the surface of N, S-CDs@MIP is notably rough, which was attributed to the imprinting cavities left on the polymer after the elution of amoxicillin molecules [45]. The SEM image (Fig. S1) of N, S-CDs@NIP shows that N, S-CDs@NIP have the similar morphology and size with N, S-CDs@MIP. But the surface of N, S-CDs@NIP is relatively smooth compare to N, S-CDs@MIP. In addition, to measure the specific surface area of N, S-CDs@MIP, N2 adsorption-desorption test was conducted, and the adsorption-desorption isotherms of N, S-CDs@MIP and N, S-CDs@NIP are given in Fig. S2. According to the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) model, the specific surface area is calculated as 70.604 and 6.433 m2/g for N, S-CDs@MIP and N, S-CDs@NIP, respectively. N, S-CDs@MIP possesses a significantly larger specific surface area than N, S-CDs@NIP, which is beneficial for strong adsorption and specific recognition of N, S-CDs@MIP toward target molecules [46].

To verify the successful S/N co-doping in carbon quantum dots, infrared spectra of N, S-CDs was measured. As depicted in Fig. 2a, the peak at 1640 cm−1 corresponds to the CC group, and the peak at 3450 and 3150 cm−1 corresponds to the -OH and -NH, which indicates the successful preparation of carbon quantum dots [47]. The characteristic absorption at 1400 cm−1 corresponds to the CN group, while the absorption at 1150 cm−1 corresponds to the CS group [47,48]. However, neither the sulfur source, nitrogen source, nor carbon source used in the preparation of N, S-CDs contains CN and CS groups. Thus, the appearance of the absorption peaks of CN or CS indicates the successful doping of nitrogen and sulfur into the carbon quantum dots [49]. Fig. 2b presents the infrared spectrum of N, S-CDs@MIP. The absorption at 3500 cm−1 corresponds to the -OH group from the monomers, while the absorption at 2989 and 2958 cm−1 corresponds to CH bonds. The absorption at 1726 cm−1 is due to the CO bond from both monomers and crosslinking agents, while the peak at 1451 cm−1 is attributed to the C-C=C bond from both monomers and crosslinking agents. Additionally, the absorption at 1263 and 1161 cm−1 originates from the C-O-C bond from the crosslinking agents [50]. The occurrence of the above-mentioned characteristic absorption from monomers and crosslinking agents indicates the presence of molecularly imprinted polymer in N, S-CDs@MIP. Furthermore, the absorption peak of the CN bond at 1400 cm−1 was clearly observed, confirming that N, S-CDs@MIP is a composite consisting of N, S-CDs, and molecularly imprinted polymer. Fig. 2c shows that N, S-CDs@NIP has a similar FT-IR spectrum to N, S-CDs@MIP. To validate the successful elution of amoxicillin from the N, S-CDs@MIP, the IR spectrum of the N, S-CDs@MIP before elution was measured. As seen in Fig. 2d, the characteristic peak at 2968 cm−1 corresponds to the CH group from the benzene ring. Simultaneously, the characteristic peaks of CC bonds in the aromatic ring are observed at 1580 and 1480 cm−1, which results from the benzene ring vibration of the amoxicillin molecule [51,52]. However, these characteristic peaks from amoxicillin are absent in FT-IR spectra of both N, S-CDs@MIP and N, S-CDs@NIP, demonstrating the successful elution of amoxicillin from N, S-CDs@MIP.

The fluorescence performance of N, S-CDs@MIP was explored via fluorescence spectra. From Fig. 3a, N, S-CDs@MIP possesses a maximum excitation wavelength of 355 nm and a maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of 440 nm, indicating that N, S-CDs@MIP can emit blue fluorescence under the irradiation of ultraviolet. To verify whether the prepared N, S-CDs@MIP presents a fluorescence response to amoxicillin, the fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs@MIP as well as the mixture of amoxicillin and N, S-CDs@MIP were measured under the same condition. As presented in Fig. 3b, the mixture containing N, S-CDs@MIP and amoxicillin has a significantly lower fluorescence intensity compared to N, S-CDs@MIP, indicating that amoxicillin can greatly quench the fluorescence of the probe N, S-CDs@MIP. In the previous reports, the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism for CDs and heteroatom-doped CDs includes energy transfer, internal filtering effect (IFE), and charge transfer [53–55]. For the prepared probe N, S-CDs@MIP, the possibility of energy transfer mechanism was excluded because the maximum UV absorption wavelength (254 nm) of amoxicillin significantly differs from the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength (440 nm) of N, S-CDs@MIP. Additionally, the maximum UV absorption wavelength of amoxicillin is greatly different from the maximum fluorescence excitation wavelength (355 nm) of N, S-CDs@MIP, so that amoxicillin is unable to absorb the UV light that is used to excite N, S-CDs@MIP. Therefore, the mechanism of the internal filtering effect (IFE) cannot explain the phenomenon of fluorescence of N, S-CDs@MIP. Thus, it is inferred that the fluorescence quenching of amoxicillin to N, S-CDs@MIP is perhaps attributed to selective adsorption of MIP and the charge transfer of N, S-CDs. When amoxicillin is adsorbed and bound to the imprinted cavity of N, S-CDs@MIP, the excited electrons in the conduction band of N, S-CDs transfers to the lowest unoccupied orbitals of the amoxicillin molecule, which leads to the fluorescence quenching of the N, S-CDs@MIP [55].The excellent fluorescence response ability of N, S-CDs@MIP toward amoxicillin means that N, S-CDs@MIP is a desirable fluorescence probe for the quantitative detection of amoxicillin.

Considering the possible reuse of the fluorescence probe N, S-CDs@MIP in the detection of amoxicillin, the stability of N, S-CDs@MIP was evaluated by repeatedly binding and eluting template molecule amoxicillin. As displayed in Fig, S3, after amoxicillin were eluted from N, S-CDs@MIP, the fluorescence performance of N, S-CDs@MIP was restored, suggesting that N, S-CDs@MIP still has high adsorption efficiency toward amoxicillin after eluting the template molecule. But the fluorescence response (F0/F) slightly decreases with the increasing times of reuse, which may be because the repeated elution causes a slight damage to the imprinted cavity and thus makes part of imprinted cavities unable to highly match with amoxicillin molecule. As a result, the binding capacity of the template molecule gradually decreases. However, the F0/F still retained 91.8 % of its original fluorescence response after 5 cycles, demonstrating a good reusability of N, S-CDs@MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of incubation time on fluorescence response
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, when a fluorescence probe comes into contact with the target pollutant, the fluorescence intensity of the probe will increase with the increasing contact time and ultimately reaches stability after a certain period of time. To identify the time required for the stable fluorescence signal and thus find the optimum incubation time for sample analysis, the fluorescence response (F0/F) of N, S-CDs@MIP in amoxicillin solution (50, 100, and 200 ng/mL) at different contact times were measured. As shown in Fig. 4a, the F0/F value of N, S-CDs@MIP increases with the prolonged incubation time. After 45 min, the F0/F value remains stable, indicating that the adsorption of amoxicillin molecules on N, S-CDs@MIP reaches equilibrium. Additionally, for the different concentrations of amoxicillin solution, the change in F0/F with time is almost consistent. Therefore, 45 min was identified as the optimum incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH on fluorescence response
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs@MIP enhances with the increasing pH at the range of pH 1–7. However, when the pH ≥ 8, the fluorescence intensity decreases with the rise of pH. The maximum fluorescence intensity is achieved at pH of 7. When N, S-CDs@MIP is exposed to the amoxicillin solution, its fluorescence intensity exhibits a similar variation to N, S-CDs@MIP in ultrapure water. The influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs@MIP may be due to the protonation and deprotonation of CDs [56]. The emission sites on the surface of the N, S-CDs@MIP are protonated under acidic condition while they are deprotonated under alkaline conditions, which hinders the radiation transition of the electron, and thus making the fluorescence weakened or even annihilated [57,58]. Additionally, Fig. 5b indicates that influence of pH on the fluorescence response (F0/F) present similar phenomenon to fluorescence intensity. This is because the amino group in amoxicillin molecular is protonated form with positive charge under acid condition. Thus, the repulsion between the protonated N, S-CDs@MIP and positively charged amoxicillin molecular prevent their close contact, resulting in the decrease in fluorescent quenching effect. Under alkaline conditions, the hydroxyl group in amoxicillin molecular is deprotonated form with negative charge. As a results, the deprotonated N, S-CDs@MIP cannot easily contact with the negatively charged amoxicillin molecular, bring about weak fluorescence quenching effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of temperature on fluorescence response
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 gives the fluorescence response (F0/F) of N, S-CDs@MIP at different temperature. In the range of 10–20 °C, N, S-CDs@MIP exhibits the highest fluorescence response toward amoxicillin. When the temperature was >20 °C, fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@MIP slightly decrease with the increasing temperature. However, N, S-CDs@MIP possesses high fluorescence response toward amoxicillin at 10–30 °C. It is feasible for N, S-CDs@MIP to detect amoxicillin at the room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of NOMs on fluorescence response
ce:section.ce:para: The natural organic matters (NOMs) are one of the most abundant materials in natural waters and consist mainly of humic acid and fulvic acid [59,60]. Therefore, the effect of humic acid and fulvic acid on the fluorescence response of the N, S-CDs@MIP was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 , with the increasing concentration of humic acid and fulvic acid, the fluorescence response (F0/F) increases slightly, which may be attributed to the fact that humic acid and fulvic acid possess carbonyl and hydroxyl groups that were able to interact with part of recognition sites of the imprinted polymers to form hydrogen bonds and thus cause fluorescence quenching of N, S-CDs@MIP. However, when the concentration of humic acid and fulvic acid is up to 30 μg·mL-1, the fluorescence response only increases by <5 %. Therefore, in the practical application, the effect of NOMs on the fluorescence detection of amoxicillin can be ignored.

Five kinds of typical antibiotics ampicillin (SAM), ofloxacin (OFX), ciprofloxacin (CIP), tetracycline (TC), and chloramphenicol (CPL) were chosen as the analogues to investigate the selectivity of N, S-CDs@MIP toward amoxicillin. The fluorescence responses of N, S-CDs@MIP to amoxicillin and its analogues are given in Fig. 8a. The fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@MIP to amoxicillin is dramatically higher than that to the analogues. In contrast, there is no obvious distinction in the fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@NIP to amoxicillin and its analogues, suggesting that the synthesized N, S-CDs@MIP exhibits significant selectivity toward amoxicillin, which is because the existence of the specific binding sites in N, S-CDs@MIP that are complementary to the amoxicillin brings about the selective binding of amoxicillin on N, S-CDs@MIP and the consequent fluorescence quenching. But there are no recognition sites that are complementary in shape, size, and functional groups to the analogues in N, S-CDs@MIP, which makes them difficult to be adsorbed on N, S-CDs@MIP to quench the fluorescence. Generally, the ratio of the amount of template adsorbed on the imprinted polymer to the amount of template adsorbed on the non-imprinted polymer is defined as the imprinting factor. The imprinting factor can be calculated by formula (1) [61].

F 0
/ F
NIP
− 1
Based on the F0/F value of N, S-CDs@MIP, and N, S-CDs@NIP in Fig. 5a, the imprinting factor is calculated to be 4.07, suggesting that N, S-CDs@MIP possesses good specific recognition to amoxicillin.

It is noteworthy that N, S-CDs@MIP also exhibits a strong fluorescence response to ampicillin. This is attributed to the highly similar molecular structures of ampicillin and amoxicillin. In comparison to ampicillin, amoxicillin only has an additional hydroxyl group on its benzene ring. However, the fluorescence quenching of amoxicillin to N, S-CDs@MIP is still significantly higher than that of ampicillin, indicating that the specific recognition of N, S-CDs@MIP toward amoxicillin resulting from the imprinted cavity in N, S-CDs@MIP can prevent compounds with minor structural differences from entering N, S-CDs@MIP. Additionally, the fluorescence response of N, S-CDs toward amoxicillin and its analogues except tetracycline is very weak and shows no obvious difference, indicating non-selectivity of fluorescence response of N, S-CDs. Therefore, it is concluded that the fluorescence response selectivity of N, S-CDs@MIP toward amoxicillin is attributed to the specific recognition of amoxicillin molecularly imprinted polymer. In addition, the fluorescence response of N, S-CDs toward tetracycline is significantly higher than that of N, S-CDs@NIP to tetracycline. This phenomenon is due to two aspects. On the one hand, the maximum ultraviolet absorption wavelength (~350 nm) of tetracycline is in close proximity to the fluorescence excitation wavelength (355 nm) of N, S-CDs, which enable tetracycline to absorb the ultraviolet light used to excite N, S-CDs, bringing about a strong fluorescence quenching of N, S-CDs [54]. However, the maximum UV absorption wavelengths of SAM, OFX, CIP, and CPL is located near 254 nm, 294 nm, 278 nm, and 278 nm, respectively, which is remarkedly different from the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength of N, S-CDs, making it difficult for them to quench the fluorescence of N, S-CDs by energy transfer and internal filtering effect [53,54]. Thus, compared to other pollutants, tetracycline exhibits significantly high fluorescence response to N, S-CDs. On the other hand, in N, S-CDs@NIP, N, S-CDs have been encapsulated in molecularly imprinted polymers, which making it cannot come into direct contact with tetracycline, thus greatly inhibiting internal filtration effect. Furthermore, N, S-CDs@NIP has no imprinted cavities that is highly compatible with tetracycline. It is difficult for tetracycline to adsorb onto the surface of N, S-CDs@NIP. Thus, the fluorescence quenching caused by charge transfer does not occur [55]. The above two factors result in lower fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@NIP to tetracycline than that of N, S-CDs to tetracycline.

To further assess whether the slight fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@MIP to analogues will interfere with the quantitative analysis of amoxicillin, the fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@MIP in the mixed solution containing amoxicillin and its analogues was measured. As displayed in Fig. 8b, when ofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, and chloramphenicol exist in the amoxicillin solution, the fluorescence response is almost identical to the blank sample, suggesting that ofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, and chloramphenicol will not interfere with the fluorescence determination of amoxicillin. Ampicillin and tetracycline exhibit a slight interference for the determination of amoxicillin, and ampicillin has a larger interference than tetracycline.

Various metal ions are commonly present in natural water bodies, and CDs are often used for the fluorescence probe of metal ions [62]. Therefore, the influence of typical metal ions (Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Na+, and K+) on the fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@MIP was explored to further confirm the specificity and applicability of N, S-CDs@MIP for the fluorescence determination of amoxicillin. Taking the diverse types and concentrations of metal ions in different natural water samples into consideration, the concentration of metal ions was chosen to be 10 times that of amoxicillin. As shown in Fig. 8c, the presence of Fe2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Na+, and K+ in amoxicillin solution only exerts an extremely minor influence on the fluorescence response, revealing that these metal ions will not cause significant interference. Nevertheless, Fe3+ has an obvious influence on the fluorescence response toward amoxicillin, which is due to the high sensitivity of CDs to Fe3+ ions [63]. Therefore, it is necessary to inhibit the fluorescence quenching action of Fe3+ to N, S-CDs@MIP, thus eliminating the interference from Fe3+ during the determination of amoxicillin. Considering that Fe2+ does not affect the fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@MIP toward amoxicillin, reducing Fe3+ to Fe2+ should be a rapid and facile method to eliminate Fe3+ interference.

Thus, ascorbic acid (AA) solution (100 μg/mL) was put into 50 mL amoxicillin solution as well as amoxicillin solution containing Fe3+ for the elimination of Fe3+ interference, respectively. As presented in Fig. 8d, the fluorescence response decreases with the addition of ascorbic acid. When the dosage of the ascorbic acid solution is >3 mL, the fluorescence response is begins to stabilize. From Fig. 8e, the addition of ascorbic acid in amoxicillin solution does not affect the fluorescence response. Moreover, after 3 mL of ascorbic acid was used to eliminate Fe3+ interference, N, S-CDs@MIP exhibited an identic fluorescence response compared with N, S-CDs@MIP without Fe3+. Therefore, ascorbic acid can effectively eliminate the interference from Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of fluorescence analysis method of amoxicillin
ce:section.ce:para: To realize the fluorescence analysis of amoxicillin with N, S-CDs@MIP as a probe, the quantitative relation between the fluorescence response of the probe and the amoxicillin concentration is required to be established. Fig. 9a presents the fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs@MIP in an amoxicillin solution with various concentrations. It can be observed that the amoxicillin with various concentrations shows an identic maximum emission wavelength, meaning that amoxicillin concentration has no impact on maximum emission wavelength. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity decreases as the concentration of amoxicillin increases, implying the quenching level of amoxicillin to fluorescence is dependent on amoxicillin concentration. To further determine the quantitative relation between the fluorescence response (F0/F) and amoxicillin concentration (C), the calibration curve was plotted. As shown in Fig. 9b, F0/F shows an excellent linearity with amoxicillin concentration in the concentration region of 0–200 mg/L. The calibration curve equation is: F0/F = 0.0034C + 0.9806 with a multiple correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9949. The linear range of the calibration curve is 5.8–200 ng/mL. According to the 3σ criterion (3σ/k) [64], the detection limit is obtained as 1.17 ng/mL, where k represents the slope of the calibration curve, and σ indicates the standard deviation of the blank measurements (n = 20). Additionally, the amoxicillin solution (100 ng/mL) was measured five times under the same conditions, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be <3 %, indicating high precision in the detection of amoxicillin. The synthesized N, S-CDs@MIP from three different batches were respectively added to 100 ng/mL of amoxicillin solution, and the measurement of fluorescence response was conducted under the same condition. The RSD of <5 % was obtained, suggesting good reproducibility of the synthesis of N, S-CDs@MIP. In addition, the fluorescence response of amoxicillin with various concentration using N, S-CDs@NIP as probe was measured. As shown in Fig. S4, similar to N, S-CDs@MIP, the F0/F value of N, S-CDs@NIP toward amoxicillin also present linearity with amoxicillin concentration. However, fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@NIP is dramatically lower than that of N, S-CDs@MIP, indicating that N, S-CDs@NIP has a low sensitivity for the quantitative detection of amoxicillin. Therefore, N, S-CDs@NIP is not suitable as fluorescent probes for detecting amoxicillin.

To testify the adaptability and accuracy of the prepared fluorescence probe in the quantitative analysis of amoxicillin in environmental water samples, three samples from different sections of the Yangtze River were measured. Each water sample was spiked with various concentrations (25, 50, 100, 150 mg/L) of amoxicillin standard solution and was analyzed with N, S-CDs@MIP as fluorescence probes, respectively. The amoxicillin content in water samples and recoveries is listed in Table 1 . As presented in Table 1, the fluorescence analysis of environmental water samples has high accuracy and precision with good recoveries ranging from 83.6 % to 104.7 % and low RSD form ranging from 2.3 % to 5.1 %., and the amoxicillin from the three tested river samples was not detected. It is concluded that the established analysis method with N, S-CDs@MIP as fluorescence probe is reliable and applicable for the detection of trace amoxicillin in environmental water samples.

Table 2 gives the comparison between fluorescence detection methods based on N, S-CDs@MIPs established in this work as well as some currently reported methods for amoxicillin detection. As seen from Table 2, compared to reported fluorescence probes, such as the pure or doped CDs, semiconductor QD (TGA-VS2QDs), and electrochemical sensor, the developed fluorescence probes N, S-CDs@MIP has advantages of lower detection limit and high selectivity. Compared with the conventional detection method including HPLC-MS, HPLC-UV, the established fluorescence detection method shows lower detection limit and high selectivity. Moreover, the developed detection method required no expensive instrument and time-consuming pretreatment, exhibiting the virtues of low cost, easy operation, saving time, and environmental friendliness. In a word, the established fluorescence detection method exhibits the advantages of high selectivity, wide linear range, good precision, low detection limit, low cost, and easy-to-implement. Consequently, it is a promising analysis method for the quick determination of trace amoxicillin.

A novel fluorescent nanoprobe N, S-CDs@MIP based on N/S co-doped CDs combined with amoxicillin MIPs was successfully synthesized. The synthesized N, S-CDs@MIP possesses a maximum excitation wavelength of 355 nm and maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of 440 nm and exhibits highly specific recognition and significantly selective fluorescence response toward the template molecule amoxicillin with a higher imprinting factor of 4.07. The optimum incubation time and pH condition for the fluorescence response is 45 min and pH = 7, respectively. The established fluorescence analysis method based on N, S-CDs@MIP for the quantitative determination of amoxicillin possesses a calibration curve equation: F0/F = 0.0034C + 0.9806 with a multiple correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9949, a linear range of the calibration curve of 5.8–200 ng/mL, a detection limit of 1.17 ng/mL, and an RSD of <3 %. The established method was put into use in detecting the amoxicillin content in the real environmental water samples, showing a high accuracy and precision with good recoveries of 83.6 % ~ 104.7 % and low RSD form ranging from 2.3 % to 5.1 %. The developed detection method using N, S-CDs@MIP as fluorescence probe is a promising analysis method for the quick determination of trace amoxicillin. This work provides a new strategy for the development of a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence analysis method for detecting trace antibiotics in the environment.
